coredata.dc:title: Ionic transport mechanisms in inorganic solid electrolytes: Interface, NMR and DNP studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mbayachi, Vestince Balidi
coredata.dc:description: Advancements in electronic devices such as drones, hybrid, and electric cars have prompted demand for superior next-generation battery technologies, with research focusing on solid electrolytes (SEs). SEs are categorized into organic (polymer) and inorganic (ceramic) electrolytes, with this review focusing on inorganic solid electrolytes (ISEs). The main obstacles to high-performing ISEs are poor ionic conductivity at room temperature and high impedance at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Many strategies to improve the conductivity and interface have been attempted and are highlighted in detail in this review. This review commences by detailing the ion conduction mechanisms in ISEs, including halides, phosphates (NASICON), oxides (perovskite, antiperovskite, and garnet), and sulfides (argyrodite-type, LGPS-type, and LISICON). The review further explores the influence of defect chemistry, elemental substitution, ion migration pathways, ion doping, and phase stability on ionic mobility and interface in ISEs. Theoretical calculation and experimental characterization are discussed in parallel to give a comprehensive and deep grasp of ion movement and interfaces. Additionally, various nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques have been explored, such as NMR relaxometry to examine both slow and rapid bulk ion transport in ISEs, PFG-NMR to investigate ion self-diffusivity, and 2D NMR exchange spectroscopy (2D EXSY) to study ion exchange mechanisms. The review concludes by discussing dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) as a hyperpolarization technique to enhance NMR sensitivity for electrode-electrolyte interfacial studies, and by proposing future research directions for ISEs.

head.ce:title: Ionic transport mechanisms in inorganic solid electrolytes: Interface, NMR and DNP studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advancements in electronic devices such as drones, hybrid, and electric cars have prompted demand for superior next-generation battery technologies, with research focusing on solid electrolytes (SEs). SEs are categorized into organic (polymer) and inorganic (ceramic) electrolytes, with this review focusing on inorganic solid electrolytes (ISEs). The main obstacles to high-performing ISEs are poor ionic conductivity at room temperature and high impedance at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Many strategies to improve the conductivity and interface have been attempted and are highlighted in detail in this review. This review commences by detailing the ion conduction mechanisms in ISEs, including halides, phosphates (NASICON), oxides (perovskite, antiperovskite, and garnet), and sulfides (argyrodite-type, LGPS-type, and LISICON). The review further explores the influence of defect chemistry, elemental substitution, ion migration pathways, ion doping, and phase stability on ionic mobility and interface in ISEs. Theoretical calculation and experimental characterization are discussed in parallel to give a comprehensive and deep grasp of ion movement and interfaces. Additionally, various nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques have been explored, such as NMR relaxometry to examine both slow and rapid bulk ion transport in ISEs, PFG-NMR to investigate ion self-diffusivity, and 2D NMR exchange spectroscopy (2D EXSY) to study ion exchange mechanisms. The review concludes by discussing dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) as a hyperpolarization technique to enhance NMR sensitivity for electrode-electrolyte interfacial studies, and by proposing future research directions for ISEs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) is a powerful technique for studying ion transport in inorganic solid electrolytes. By coupling NMR with Dynamic Nuclear Polarization (DNP), interfacial properties can also be effectively explored.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their commercialization in 1991, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been extensively used in portable gadgets, hybrid or electric vehicles, and many electronic devices. They also hold significant potential for large-scale energy storage, ideal for fluctuating power sources like solar and wind [1–3]. With increasing demand for electrification, the use of LIBs is growing rapidly. Thus, these batteries are anticipated to provide long life cycles, high energy density, maintain safety, and possess lightweight features [4,5]. The growth of advanced devices like drones has sparked a need for improved battery technology that exceeds the nearing physicochemical restrictions of existing LIBs [6]. Current LIBs use liquid electrolytes, including flammable organic solvents like ethylene carbonate and propylene carbonate, which pose safety issues due to leakage, vaporization, and decomposition [7–9].

Research efforts have recently focused on creating safer, high-energy density storage technologies for the next generation batteries [10]. All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are one class of the proposed next-generation technology since they consist entirely of solid electrolytes and electrodes [11]. Switching from liquid electrolytes (LEs) to solid electrolytes (SEs) has sparked significant interest due to safety, longevity, and performance benefits. SEs reduce risks of flammability and leakage, operate efficiently over a broad temperature range, offer enhanced thermal stability, suppress lithium dendrite growth, and accommodate the use of high-voltage cathode materials due to their large electrochemical windows [12–14]. Furthermore, SEs could facilitate the use of lithium metal anode, significantly enhancing battery energy density. This is due to the exceptional theoretical specific capacity of lithium metal (3860 mA h g−1), low electrochemical potential (-3.04 V against SHE), and low density (0.53 g cm−3 at RT) [15].

Studies on ASSBs began by searching for appropriate materials that can function as SEs with superior ionic conductivity (σLi+ > 0.1 mS cm−1) [5]. SEs can be categorized as organic and inorganic electrolytes [16]. Organic electrolytes mainly comprise polymers, while inorganic consist of halides, sulfides, and ceramics (phosphates and oxide-based) electrolytes. Until now, developing effective SEs has been a significant challenge for ASSBs because of high interfacial resistance and poor ionic conductivity at room temperature (RT), which has hindered their commercialization [17]. Current inorganic solid electrolytes (ISEs) face several drawbacks, including high manufacturing or sintering temperature (>900 °C), consistency problems related to defect chemistry, metastability, grain boundary obstruction, and electrochemical instability [18]. Due to severe interfacial resistance, these drawbacks lead to increased polarization, reduced capacity, and low power and cycling abilities [19]. The difficulty of integrating ISEs with electrodes results in significant interfacial contact resistance. Thus, using thinner SE membranes and operating the ASSB at higher temperatures (>80 °C) could be advantageous in improving both ionic conductivity and interfacial resistance [20].

Relations between computational and experimental studies have been adopted to study the synthesis, properties, and structure of ISEs. To develop efficient electrolytes, it is necessary to comprehend Li+ transport mechanisms [21]. The study of ion transport mechanisms is a crucial scientific issue that has been overlooked due to technological constraints, time requirements, and cost [22]. Solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy uses NMR line shape and relaxation measurements to efficiently examine slow and rapid bulk ion movements in ASSBs [23,24]. Computational methods are essential for analyzing and forecasting the structure and properties of complex materials on an atomic scale. Methods like density functional theory (DFT) and bond-valence (BV) in computational chemistry have shown efficiency in studying battery material chemistry [25,26]. The BV method is a quick technique that allows the simulation of ion migration (diffusion) pathways and energy barriers (Ea) for a crystal structure to be completed on a computer in just a few minutes. A drawback of the BV method is that it does not provide a quantifiable value for Ea [27]. Thus, the molecular dynamics or transition-state method based on DFT can provide more reliable calculations for energy barriers [25,28]. However, their efficiency in screening materials is limited due to their high computational cost. Integrating the DFT calculations and BV methods significantly aids in rapidly screening, designing, and optimizing ISEs for ASSBs [29].

In this review, a comprehensive overview of the Li-ion transport mechanisms in various types of ISEs, including perovskite and anti-perovskite oxides (e.g., LLTO and Li3OCl, respectively), garnet oxides (e.g., LLZO), Na super-ionic conductor (NASICON e.g., LATP), thio-lithium superionic conductor (LISICON e.g., Li3PS4), sulfides (argyrodite-type Li6PS5X), and halide (e.g., Li3YCl6), have been discussed. The review further explores how factors such as point defects, chemical composition, microstructure, crystal structure, diffusion types, and pathways influence Li-ion conduction. It then addresses factors such as voids, chemical reactions, electrochemical reactions, and grain boundaries that influence interfaces. Later, it comments on strategies to improve interface and conductivity. Additionally, it highlights the use of spin-lattice relaxation 7Li NMR to examine diffusion process mechanisms (both slow and rapid bulk Li-ion movements) in ISEs, PFG-NMR to study ion self-diffusivity, and 2D exchange spectroscopy (2D EXSY) techniques to observe Li-ion exchange across the interfaces. However, considering the low sensitivity of NMR and the limited number of surface atoms, dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) is also reviewed as a hyperpolarization technique to enhance sensitivity, allowing for the study of electrode-electrolyte interface.

Li-ion transport in ceramics is primarily determined by the defects within the ceramic crystals [30]. In perfect ceramic crystals, ionic mobility is limited [31]. Defects in ceramic crystal structures include point, planar, line, electron, and volume defects. Point defects are the most researched and recognized type, and they play a vital role in the ion transport mechanism in a crystal. Frenkel first described point defects in 1926 [32]. They can be stoichiometric defects, Frenkel defects, and Schottky defects. Stoichiometric defects, also known as intrinsic defects, include vacancy defects (vacant sites in the crystal lattice) and interstitial defects (ion movement in the interstitial site of the crystal lattice). Frenkel defects are also called interstitial or dislocation defects and occur when a cation moves from its original lattice site to an interstitial position, leaving a vacancy in the crystal or lattice structure. Schottky defects are a type of vacancy defect whereby cations and anions are both missing, yet the crystal remains neutral ( Fig. 1a). Impurity defects occur when a foreign ion replaces a parent ion in an interstitial site [33].

Inorganic solid electrolytes (ISEs) are crystalline structures composed of a fixed arrangement of ions, an immobile framework, and a Li-ion sublattice. The Li+ mobility is significantly impacted by its interaction with the immobile framework. Similarly, the migration path and dimensionality of the Li-ion are affected by its interaction with the fixed ion arrangement [22]. Point defects in ISEs can be caused by various factors, including: (i) Numerous vacancies exist when fractional occupancy of ion sites exists. (ii) Through substitution or doping of elements in ISEs, which leads to various point defects. (iii) When ISEs are exposed to extreme operation conditions, such as charging-discharging processes, defect reactions can occur [34,35]. In crystal materials with ordered structures, ionic mobility involves random movement along the static energy path of individual defects once point defects occur. However, individual defects cannot explain ion transport in materials with disordered structures, such as disordered ionic crystals, nanosized systems, and ion-conducting glasses. Thus, the charge carriers interact among themselves and with the transport matrix during ion transport [21].

Three key elements - carrier type, diffusion pathway, and diffusion type - regulate Li-ion movement within the ISE materials (Fig. 1c). Point defects decide the concentration and type of carriers, which influences ionic mobility. The arrangement of anions has a strong impact on the diffusion pathways of Li-ions. Three types of Li-ion diffusion (interstitial directly hopping, vacancy directly hopping, and interstitial knock-off) also affect ionic conductivity (Fig. 1b). The Interstitial directly hopping mechanism utilizes Frenkel defects to diffuse Li+ ions between the interstitial skeleton of ceramic molecules by repeatedly displacing them into adjacent open sites [22]. Schottky defects facilitate the vacancy directly hopping mechanism process, resulting in abundant vacancies that Li+ ions hop through. Each movement of Li+ ions generates a new vacancy at its original position, thus continuing the cycle [21]. Interstitial knock-off involves the diffusion of ions by continuously displacing them to an adjacent lattice site instead of hopping through vacant spaces between lattice sites [34]. Considering that Schottky defects are empty spaces in the lattice while Frenkel defects are ions that occupy space between atoms, both these defects are thermally induced. This implies that an increase in temperature increases the number of defects. Thus, the conductivity can be improved by raising the temperature [36].

Energy barriers and anion sublattice affect the diffusion pathways. Fig. 1d depicts the migration of Li+ and Na+ in model systems with no cations in body-centered cubic (bcc), face-centered cubic (fcc), and hexagonal close-packed (hcp) anion sublattice [37]. In a bcc anion sublattice like LGPS and Li7P3S11, Li+ occupies tetrahedral (Tet) sites. They move by passing through an anion-triangle bottleneck to another adjacent tetrahedral site, resulting in a low Ea of 0.12 eV for Li+ migration. Conversely, in fcc and hcp anion frameworks, typically found in LiCl and LiBr superionic conductors Li3MX6, Li+ migration exhibits low Ea ranging from 0.2–0.3 eV.

In polycrystalline ceramic electrolytes, the total ionic conductivity is determined by its grain boundaries. The grain boundaries differ in structure and composition from the grain bulk crystals. Li-ion mobility in conducting ceramics depends on migration pathways in grain bulk, pores, and potential barriers in grain boundaries [38,39]. The resistance of intergrain boundaries is typically greater than the resistance of intragrains. However, three Li-ion transport pathways can be observed in polycrystalline ceramics. They include intergranular transport between grains, intragranular transport within grains, and transport within grain boundaries (Fig. 1e) [40]. Grain boundary resistance may significantly reduce the total conductivity to half that of bulk conductivity [41]. Thus, reducing grain boundary resistance is essential to enhance total conductivity.

Several techniques have been introduced to lower the grain boundary resistance. Among them include: (i) Optimization of total conductivity through heat treatment [42]. (ii) Traditional cold-pressing of SE to decrease grain-boundary resistance [43]. (iii) Optimization of synthesis conditions of ISEs through controlled grain size, sintering, and doping with elements such as aluminum [44,45]. (iv) Addition of a secondary phase at grain boundaries, which can be through the introduction of additives such as silica to induce an amorphous boundary layer [46]. The second phases are believed to create a space charge layer, change compositions, and alter grain boundaries, which would be expected to influence ionic conductivity (Fig. 1f) [36,46]. Additionally, the reduction of the grain boundary resistance results in the homogenization of the grain surface, the addition of Li-ions in the grain boundaries, and the tightly binding of grains due to the presence of an amorphous layer [39].

The Na super-ionic conductor (NASICON) framework is a phosphate-type SE derived from NaM2(PO4)3, where M represents Ge, Ti, or Zr [22]. NaM2(PO4)3 structure, in which Na and Li are interchangeable, forms a rhombohedral framework with a R3c̅ space group. The configuration consists of M2(PO4)3 units, each comprising two MO6 octahedra and three PO4 tetrahedra that share O atoms [47]. LiM2(PO4)3 is classified into three categories including LiZr2(PO4)3 (LZP), where M = Zr, LiTi2(PO4)3 (LTP), where M = Ti, and LiGe2(PO4)3 (LGP), where M = Ge [21]. The LiTi2(PO4)3 skeleton ( Fig. 2a) is ideal for Li-ion transport, although its conductivity is low due to poor sintering ability. By partially substituting Ti4+ with Al3+ in the Li1 + x Ti2 − x Al x (PO4)3 system, ionic conductivity can be increased by a factor of 10−3 because of the smaller unit-cell dimensions [48].

In the rhombohedral structure, Li+ are positioned at sites bordered by six oxygen atoms at the inversion symmetric Wyckoff position 6b (M1). Another structural site for Li atoms (M2, 18e) exists between two M1 positions, featuring an unorthodox 10-fold oxygen coordination. Both M1 and M2 sites are depicted in (Fig. 2b). They alternate along the conduction channels, forming a 3D network throughout the crystal (Fig. 2c). Compositions with larger tetravalent cations, such as Sn and Zr, also display low-temperature triclinic phase of lower symmetry due to Li ions displacement. In this triclinic structure, the Li+ shifts to M1/2 positions between M1 and M2 and is coordinated by four oxygen atoms. Thermal activation prompts ions to hop between empty or interstitial sites, driving their migration in crystals [49,50].

While NASICON-type solid electrolytes offer high ionic conductivity, air and water stability, and a broad electrochemical window (⁓ 6 V vs. Li∣Li+), they lack stability against Li metal anode [51]. However, attempts have been made to improve Li anode with NASICON electrolytes. A study by He et al. [52] showed that applying a thin ZnO layer on LATP enhances the interface stability of Li-LATP. As a result, the cells assembled by protected Li had a longer life cycle and a consistently high Coulombic efficiency. Another study by Cipollone et al. [53] introduced a 3D porous copper scaffold to increase the interface stability and suppress Li dendrite growth between the NASICON and Li anode. They found that a thin CuO interlayer further improved the Li-LATP interfacial contact, minimizing the side reactions at the interface.

Halide electrolytes are favored for SE technology due to their inherent chemical characteristics surpassing chalcogen alternatives like oxygen and sulfur [54]. Firstly, single-charged halogen ions interact less strongly with Li ions than divalent sulfur or oxygen anions. As a result, lithium ions mobility in halide is faster, leading to increased ionic conductivity. Secondly, the ionic radii of halogen anions in picometers (F− = 122 pm, Cl− = 167 pm, Br− = 182 pm, and I− = 202 pm) are typically larger than those of sulfide and oxide anions (O2− = 126 pm and S2− = 170 pm) [55]. This suggests that halide electrolytes possess longer ionic bonds than oxides and sulfides, potentially resulting in greater ion mobility and enhanced deformability. Thirdly, halide anions, particularly F− and Cl−, possess a greater electrochemical redox potential than sulfide and oxide anions, thereby providing superior oxidative stability [56]. Despite being studied since the 1930s, halide electrolytes have not gained much attention due to their poor ionic conductivity at RT [57–60].

Asano and his team in 2018 conducted a comprehensive study of the halide electrolytes, Li3YCl6 and Li3YBr6. They discovered that these electrolytes possess high RT ionic conductivity exceeding 1 Ms/cm, excellent voltage stability, substantial deformability, and satisfactory resistance to dry air [54]. Various halide electrolytes have been synthesized to date, including those spanning from fluorides to iodines. Examples of these include Li3ScCl6, Li3GaF6, Li3InCl6, Li3LaI6, Li3ErCl6, Li3AlF6, Li3YCl6, Li3InBr6, Li3HoBr6, Li3HoCl6, and Li3LaI6.

The general formula of a halide electrolyte is Li3MX6, where M is a trivalent rare earth metal introduced in the LiX structure through doping, while X is F, Cl, Br, or I. Li3MX6 structures depend on the ionic sizes of positively and negatively charged ions, their polarity, and the method of their ionic arrangement (ionic packing styles) [56]. According to the ionic packing law, the stability of halide SEs depends on the close proximity of cations and anions. Essentially, the radius ratio between cations and anions (r+/r−) needs to satisfy specific requirements, as depicted in Fig. 3a. The ionic packing law suggests that most fluoride Li-ion conductors typically form a LiMF4 phase. This occurs when the M ion occupies the cubic site of the F− framework (MF8 cube), and the radius ratio of M3+/F− is frequently above 0.732. Compared to fluoride ISEs, chloride, bromide, and iodide superionic conductors generally create a stable Li3MX6-type structure. They share a similar local anion coordination environment of MX6 octahedrons due to their radius ratio, M3+/X−, which falls between 0.414 and 0.732 [61].

Typical ternary halide SEs, such as Li3MCl6, come in two anion sublattice structures: hexagonal close packing (hcp) (Fig. 3b) and cubic close packing (ccp) (Fig. 3c). The crystal structure is significantly influenced by the average ionic radius of metal cations, which can be categorized into three space groups including trigonal hcp (Fig. 3d), orthorhombic hcp (Fig. 3e), and monoclinic ccp (Fig. 3f) [62]. The trigonal structure (P3̅m1) features an hcp anion sublattice (hcp-T). Within this structure, M3+ cations typically occupy the octahedral sites. The ionic radius of these cations usually varies between 102 and 106.4 pm (e.g., for Tb3+ = 106.3 pm, Dy3+ = 103 pm). For instance, the ionic radius is 104.1 pm for Ho3+, and 102 pm for Er and Tm). In orthorhombic structure (Pnma) with hcp anion sublattice (hcp-O), M3+ cations with a small ionic radius of 100–102 pm (e.g., Yb3+ = 100.8 pm and Lu3+ = 100.1 pm) occupy the octahedral sites. Monoclinic structure with an ABCABC stacking of the ccp anion sublattice (ccp-M), M3+ cations with an ionic radius less than 100 pm occupy the octahedral sites (e.g., In3+ = 94 pm and Sc3+ = 88.5 pm).

The transport mechanisms of Lithium-ion in halide SEs are closely linked to their anion sublattices. Different ion transport routes are caused by different anion frameworks [63]. In an hcp anion framework like Li3YCl6 (Fig. 3g), ion diffusion is not uniform. The fastest diffusion occurs along one-dimensional c-axis channels, where Lithium ions move between neighboring face-sharing octahedral sites (Fig. 3j). These c-channels are linked via the ab planes through tetrahedral interstitial sites, creating an aeolotropic 3D diffusion network. In the ccp anion framework, such as in Li3YBr6 (Fig. 3h), Li+ ions diffuse through a 3D uniform network by hopping to other octahedral locations via Tet sites (see Fig. 3k). Regardless of whether the anion framework has an hcp or ccp sublattice, the energy needed for single Li+ migration in halide-SEs remains relatively low compared to sulfide anions in both scenarios (see Fig. 3i and l) [64]. This suggests the significant potential for achieving high ionic conductivity using halogen chemistry [54].

The general formula of a perfect garnet oxide is A3B2(XO4)3, where A ions can be Mg, Ca, La, Y, or rare metal; B site can be assigned to Al, Ga, Mn, Fe, Ge, V or Ni; X can be filled by Ge, Si or Al. The A ions of A3B2(XO4)3 structure have 8-fold coordination while B and X have 6- and 4-fold coordination, respectively [65]. Garnet lithium conductors such as Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO) exhibit two distinct phases: high-temperature cubic LLZO (c-LLZO) and low-temperature tetragonal LLZO (t-LLZO) ( Fig. 4a-c). The two garnet phases differ in lithium distribution. In the c-LLZO phase, lithium is distributed disorderedly (Fig. 4d). Whereas in the t-LLZO phase, lithium ions occupy completely tetragonal sites (24d), octahedral sites (48 g) and off-centered octahedral sites (96 h) (Fig. 4e) [66]. This means that the Li-ion sublattice of t-LLZO is always ordered (all Li sites are either occupied or empty), while in contrast, c-LLZO has a disordered Li sublattice (all Li symmetry sites are partially occupied). The c-LLZO exhibits higher ionic conductivity than t-LLZO (10−4−10−3 S/cm) at RT because of different diffusion mechanisms [66]. LLZO was found in 2007 by Murugan et al. [41] with ionic conductivity of 0.3 mS cm−1 and Ea of 0.3 eV at RT. c-LLZO structure at high temperatures is unstable and can easily be converted into a t-LLZO phase [67].

Doping is one of the techniques adopted to stabilize the c-LLZO phase lattice and enhance ionic conductivity at RT. High valence elements (Al3+, Ga3+, Ta5+, etc.) doping strategies can alter the phase structure, vacancy concentration, Li-ion content, ion occupancy, and skeleton structure of the LLZO lattice, thus increasing the lithium-ion migration pathways [68–70]. Fig. 4f shows the Li+ mobility in the LLZO lattice. Li+ migrates from one [Li2O6] octahedron to an adjacent [LiO4] tetrahedron and then to another [Li2O6] octahedron. The size bottleneck of Li+ transport is a triangle of 3 coplanar oxygen atoms from [LiO4] tetrahedral and [Li2O6] octahedral (Fig. 4g) [71]. Baral et al. [72] have suggested two migration pathways, A and B, in Fig. 4h. Li-ions in route A migrate between two octahedral sites, bypassing the shared tetrahedral one; the latter does not participate in the migration. Li-ions move along route B through triangles shared between octahedral and tetrahedral polyhedra, with Li-ions at tetrahedral sites participating in the migration.

The poor electrode-electrolyte interface of garnet electrolytes is the major hindrance to their commercialization. Li anode/garnet electrolyte interface instability is due to the poor wettability of garnet electrolyte with Li anode, Li dendritic growth, side reactions between Li anode and garnet electrolyte, and between the dopants with garnet electrolyte [65]. The poor wettability of Li anode is partially due to the presence of contaminants such as LiOH and Li2CO3 on its surface. The absence of stable physical contact between garnet electrolyte and Li metal during cycling leads to an inhomogeneous current distribution, which contributes to Li dendrite growth. Researchers have proposed strategies to address this problem, such as depositing ultrathin Al2O3 by atomic layer deposition at the interface between Li metal and garnet electrolyte [73]. Other techniques include fabricating composite anode materials, such as melting Li with GaN to form Li-Ga-N [74], and spin coating conductive layers, such as Li-Mg alloy/LiF (LMF) [75]. The high stiffness of LLZO increases the impedance, which increases the polarization of the cell. To reduce polarization, methods like 3D LLZO structuring, solidification (in-situ/ex-situ), cathode coating, and high-temperature sintering enhance the interfacial contact between garnet and cathode active materials [76]. Cathode-electrode interface (CEI) instability has also been tackled using low melting point ion-conductive additives. Li3BO3 additive has been used between the LLZO and LiCoO2 cathode interface because of its low melting temperature of 700 °C compared to calcination temperature above 1000 °C for LLZO [77].

Perovskite oxides have a structure formula ABX3, where A is an alkaline-earth metal or rare earth metal such as Ca, Sr, or La, B is an aluminum or transition metal, and X is oxygen [78]. An ideal perovskite structure has a cubic unit cell and Pm3̅m space group. It consists of A-ions at the corners, B-ions at the center, and oxygen at the face-centres. Fourquet et al. [79] proposed the current accepted tetragonal Li3xLa(2/3)-x□(1/3)-2xTiO3 (LLTO) model based on TEM and XRD studies ( Fig. 5a). They deduced that the distortion in TiO6 octahedra occurs along the c-axis, featuring one short Ti−O2 bond (approximately 1.8 Å), one longer Ti−O1 bond (around 2 Å), and four identical Ti−O3 bonds (each around 1.94 Å). A and B ions consist of 12-fold and 6-fold coordination, respectively, and they share corners (Fig. 5b). The lithium–lanthanum–titanates (LLTO) have an excellent bulk ionic conductivity of approximately 1 mS cm−1 at RT, making it one of the most attractive ISE. LLTO comprises two phases: α-LLTO, which has a cubic Pm3̅m structure at a high temperature, and β-LLTO, which takes on a tetragonal P4/mmm form at lower temperatures [80].

Li-ion mobility in LLTO occurs through vacancy directly hopping mechanism. Lithium insertion into the crystal alters lithium concentrations and vacancies [81]. The ionic conductivity of LLTO is determined by three key factors: the cation size of the A-site ion (bottleneck size), the concentrations of Li and vacancy, and the properties of the B–O bond. The crystal structure of tetragonal LLTO features a significant number of A-site vacancies. These vacancies facilitate Li+ mobility via a vacancy hopping mechanism through a square planar bottleneck comprised of four O2- ions between two adjacent A sites [82]. Conductivity data fits a Vogel-Tamman-Fulcher (VTF) type relationship when the temperature exceeds 127 °C, indicating a thermally assisted conduction mechanism [83]. The VTF equation was initially utilized to illustrate the temperature-related ionic conductivity in polymer electrolytes [84]. The VTF behavior indicates that ionic conduction occurs by rotating or tilting the TiO6 octahedra. This process opens and closes the bottleneck for the lithium-ion to move into an adjacent A-site vacancy, which results in a positive activation volume (Fig. 5e) [85].

X sites of perovskite-type fast ion conductors are occupied by O2- ion. Driven by these materials, Zhao et al. [86] suggested a new family of Lithium-rich anti-perovskite (LiRAP) known as Li3OX (where X represents Cl or Br), which are rapid Lithium-ion conductors (Fig. 5c). The LiRAP structure can be easily modified by substitution by replacing Cl anions with larger Br anions at the dodecahedral site [87]. Three techniques to alter the anti-perovskite structure include blending (mixing), doping (introducing impurities), and depletion (reducing components). The substitution impacts structures in two ways: (i) it adjusts the vacant concentration and ionic hopping through the Schottky route; (ii) it modifies the lattice volume and the size of the bottleneck for Li-ion diffusion [88]. Emly et al. [89] proposed a mechanism of interstitial migration with a dumbbell structure (Fig. 5f). The activation energy for this migration in Li3OCl was found to be 0.17 eV, which is lower than the 0.26 eV found in a previous experiment by Zhao et al. [86]. However, the formation energy of the Frenkel defect (interstitial Li-ion) within a 6 × 6×6 supercell was considerably high at 1.94 eV.

Lu et al. [90] explored three defect types: Li2O Schottky, LiCl Schottky, and Li interstitial with a substitutional O defect on a Cl site. Their findings indicated that the LiCl-deficient type was most favorable for Li-ion migration due to its low activation energy. Consequently, they suggested Li vacancy hopping as the primary diffusion mechanism in Li3OCl. Using classical atomistic quasi-static calculations, Mouta et al. [91] further studied the diffusion mechanism in Li3OCl (Fig. 5g). They found that the formation energy of LiCl-deficiency was 0.93 eV at 0 K and 1.01 eV between 300 K and 500 K. Additionally, the formation energy of Frenkel defects was 1.59 eV at 0 K and 1.62 eV between 300 and 550 K, which is lower than the one reported by Emly et al. (1.94 eV) [89]. Besides Li3OX, the anti-perovskite structure is also found in Li2OHX (See Fig. 5d). In the cubic Li2OHX, H-ions form straight O-H-Cl bonds in Li2OHCl while Li-ions establish straight O-Li-O bonds. Li vacancies are arranged orderly, with each O-ion surrounded by 4 Li+ and 2 H+ cations. Li-ions travel through a triangular O2- (Cl)−2 site via Li vacancies [92].

LLTO-based ceramic electrolytes offer many benefits, such as exceptional lithium single ion conductivity, excellent electrochemical stability (>8 V), negligible electronic conductivity, sturdiness in wet and dry atmospheres, and a wide thermal stability range (4 K to 1600 K). However, two major hurdles remain, which include instability against a Li metal and low grain-boundary conductivity (<10−5 S cm−1) [93]. Various strategies have been adopted to address the low grain-boundary conductivity, such as doping A site with large rare-earth, shortening the B–O interatomic distance by replacing B sites with small ionic radius elements such as aluminum, increasing sintering temperature, and fabrication of LLTO in the amorphous form.

The ionic conductivity of a bottleneck can be enhanced by adding a large alkaline-earth or rare-earth metal ions in the A site. The bigger the bottleneck, the greater the conductivity [94]. Doping LLTO with divalent alkali ions, such as Sr2+, creates more A-site vacancies, increasing the lattice parameter compared to La3+ or Li+ [95]. Besides, Al substitution for titanium at the B site improved Li+ mobility. This is achieved through the shortening of the B–O interatomic distance due to the small ionic radius (0.535 Å) in aluminum. Furthermore, oxygen vacancy creation in LLTO reduces the activation energy of Li-ion transport within and between grains, hence improving its ionic conductivity [96,97]. An increase in the sintering temperature may reduce the grain boundary resistance. This is due to the increase in grain size and decrease in grain boundary regions, resulting in increased ionic conductivity [98]. Synthesis of LLTO in the amorphous form removes their structural disorders and the grain boundary elements [99].

Sulfide ISEs demonstrate an ionic conductivity between 10−4 and 10−3 S/cm at RT due to low electronegativity and large ionic radius in sulfur. Compared to the Li-O bond, the lower electronegativity in sulfur weakens the Li-S bond strength, resulting in an increased number of mobile Li ions in sulfide electrolytes. Furthermore, the increased radius of sulfur ions creates a larger pathway for lithium ions, enhancing their movement [21]. Sulfide materials are also more malleable and softer than oxides, allowing them to be densely packed by cold pressing, reducing grain boundary resistance [100]. Sulfide electrolytes can be classified into three categories: crystalline, glass (amorphous), and glass-ceramics (partially crystalline), based on their structural traits [78]. Sulfide glasses typically have ionic conductivities higher than their crystalline counterparts due to the absence of grain boundary resistance [101]. Common sulfide-based SEs include glassy LPS systems, glass ceramics derived from LPS, argyrodite Li6PS5X (X = Br, Cl, or I), Li11–xM2–xP1+xS12 (M = Ge, Si, or Sn), and LISICONs [102].

The ionic conductivity in glassy electrolytes can be improved to approximately 10−3 S cm−1 at RT when lithium ortho-oxo salt (such as Li4SiO4 or Li3PO4) or lithium halide salt (LiX, where X = F, Cl, Br, I) are added [103]. Through heat treatment, glass-ceramics are produced by crystallizing the parent glass, resulting in both crystalline and amorphous areas. Sulfide glass-ceramics typically have higher ionic conductivities than glassy sulfides due to the superionic phases in the glass. These conductive paths are preserved through interconnected amorphous regions [104]. (100-x) Li2S–xP2S5 (LPS) is a typical glassy sulfide. Its highest conductivity is achieved when the P2S5 composition ranges from 20 % to 30 % [105]. LPS demonstrates an ion conductivity of approximately 10−4 S cm−1. Glass-ceramic, Li3PS4 (x = 25), is highly compatible with lithium metal and exists in three distinct crystal phases: α-Li3PS4, β-Li3PS4, and γ-Li3PS4. At 500 K, β-Li3PS4 demonstrated an ionic conductivity of 3.0 × 10−2 S cm−1, which is higher than α-Li3PS4 and γ-Li3PS4 [106]. Li7P3S11 (with x = 30) showcased an ionic conductivity exceeding 10−3 S cm−1 at RT [107].

Lithium argyrodites Li6PS5X (X = Cl, Br, I) is the commonly recognized crystalline sulfide. The li6PS5X crystal structure has a cubic F43m face-centered (fc) symmetry ( Fig. 6a). The S2- and Cl−/Br− anions construct a fcc lattice at the 4a and 4d sites. Simultaneously, PS4 3- tetrahedra occupy the octahedral sites with P at the 4b location. In Li6PS5X, the S2- and X− anions can be located in three distinctive crystallographic positions. Specifically, the 16e site is completely filled by S2- anions, while the 4a and 4b sites are jointly occupied by both X− and S2- anions [108]. Li-ions in Li6PS5Cl are exclusively found in the 24 g positions within the 48h–24g–48 h′ split site. However, in compounds where X is Br and I, these Li-ions are shared across both 24 g sites and 48 h positions. In the lithium argyrodite structure, three distinct types of lithium-ion transport mechanisms have been identified. These include short-range back-and-forth transitions (48h-48h), short-range intra-cage transitions among different 48 h pairs, and long-range inter-cage transitions that link the four cages in each Li6PS5X unit cell (Fig. 6b and c) [109]. The diffusion of Li, either intra-cage or inter-cage (48h-48h) along a direct or curved path, may be influenced by neighboring S2- anions of a PS4 3- tetrahedron. The jump distance is directly related to the lattice constant and halogen substitution.

Li10GeP2S12 (LGPS) is a superionic conductor that was first reported by Kamaya et al. in 2011 and shows the highest ionic conductivity (12 mS cm−1 at RT), surpassing some conventional liquid electrolytes [110]. LGPS crystal structure features a 3D framework made up of (Ge0.5P0.5)S4 tetrahedra, LiS4 tetrahedra, PS4 tetrahedra, and LiS6 octahedra (Fig. 6d). This framework forms a 1D Li conduction pathway along the c-axis. The LiS6 octahedra and (Ge0.5P0.5)S4 tetrahedra share edges, creating 1D chains, which are connected by PS4 tetrahedra (Fig. 6e). Lithium conduction occurs along chains of LiS4 tetrahedra at the 16 h and 8 f sites, with Li-ions exhibiting highly anisotropic thermal vibrations (Fig. 6f). The LiS6 octahedra at the 4d sites remain inactive for Li-ion mobility. Neutron diffraction shows Li-ions partially occupy 16 h and 8 f sites, confirming the presence of 1D conduction routes along the c-axis.

Mo et al. [111] used first-principle calculations to predict that LGPS behaves as a 3D conductor. Using ab initio MD simulations, they confirmed fast 1D Li diffusion along the c-axis channel (Fig. 6g) and predicted two additional diffusion routes in the ab plane (Fig. 6h and i). Although diffusion in the ab plane is slower, it still contributes to the overall performance of LGPS. One pathway connects LiS4 tetrahedra diagonally in the ab plane, with space between (Ge0.5P0.5)S4 and PS4 tetrahedra (Fig. 6h). Another pathway links LiS6 octahedra to the c-axis diffusion channel (Fig. 6i), connecting LiS4 tetrahedra to LiS6 octahedra, which Kamaya et al. initially considered inactive for diffusion. The predicted ionic conductivity in the ab plane was 0.9 mS/cm at RT.

A significant disadvantage of LGPS is its high cost, which results from using Ge metal, prompting research into substituting Ge with Sn or Si. Li10SnP2S12 (LSnPS) is a more affordable alternative. However, it has a higher grain boundary resistance than LGPS, resulting in a lower ionic conductivity of 4 mS/cm−1 [112]. Replacing 30 % Sn with Si to form Li10Sn0.7Si0.3P2S12 reduced grain boundary resistance, improving the conductivity to 8 mS/cm−1 [113]. Another cost-effective option, Li10SiP2S12 (LSiPS), has a smaller lattice parameter than LGPS and has an ionic conductivity of 2.3 mS/cm−1 [114].

Although sulfide electrolytes boast high ionic conductivity, they face significant setbacks. These include poor chemical stability in air and interfacial issues such as sulfide ISE–electrode side reactions, poor electrolyte-electrode contact, and Li dendrite growth during charging and discharging. Sulfur, when exposed to moisture, undergoes hydrolysis, which results in the production of harmful H2S [115]. Applying MxOy nanoparticles like Fe2O3, ZnO, and Bi2O3 to sulfide ISEs can efficiently curb this H2S generation [116]. These MxOy nanoparticles act as adsorbents, eliminating the H2S produced during the hydrolysis. Side reactions occur due to the low electrochemical stability of sulfide. Specifically, S2− oxidizes at the cathode side (≈2 V vs. Li+/Li), and P5+ reduces at the anode side (≈1 V vs. Li+/Li) [117].

To enhance the interface between the sulfide ISE and the Li metal anode, various interlayer materials have been introduced, including Au and Li-metal alloys such as LixAl(2 − x/3)O3(LiAlO) [118,119]. When the sulfide electrolyte interacts with oxide cathode materials like LiCoO2 and Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC), it triggers interfacial side reactions, increases interfacial resistance, and promotes elemental interdiffusion, all of which accelerate capacity degradation [120]. The space charge layer is the primary reason for the significant interfacial resistance at the cathode/sulfide interface. This layer results from the Li-ion concentration disparity between the oxide electrodes and the sulfide SE. Enhanced interfacial stability can be achieved by separating the sulfide SE from the cathode using coatings like Li4Ti5O12, LiNbO3, and Li3PO4 [121–123].

Thio-lithium superionic conductor (LISICON) material with LixM1-yM′ySz (M = Si and Ge and M′ = P, Al, Zn, Ga, and Sb) framework structure was proposed by Hong et al. [124]. Lithium thiophosphates have undergone extensive research as potential ISE by optimizing their ionic conductivity to reach as high as 3 × 10−3 S cm−1. This is achieved through specific conditions, including lengthy synthesis time, ball milling, and quenching [125]. Lithium thiophosphate, Li3PS4, is considered a more stable and highly promising compound in the Li2S–P2S5 family [126]. Li3PS4 experiences three transitions (γ → β → α) when the temperature rises. These changes are associated with the arrangement of the PS4 tetrahedron, which influences the placement of Li-ions and impacts ionic conduction. The experimental study reveals lithium ions in β-Li3PS4 exist in 8d, 4b, and 4c sites [106].

Fig. 7a illustrates the structure of γ-Li3PS4. This structure is made of hexagonally packed sulfide atoms. The PS4 and LiS4 tetrahedra are connected through a shared corner sulfide ion, and their apexes are uniformly aligned [127]. The β- Li3PS4 structure is made up of tetrahedra Li(4c)S4, Li(8d)S4, and PS4, and octahedra (Li(4b)S6). The Li(4c)S4 tetrahedra and Li(4b)S6 octahedra are alternately positioned along the b-axis, building the zigzag channels for Li+ transport in the [010] direction by sharing common edges [128]. This arrangement can be seen in Fig. 7b. The Li(8d)S4 and PS4 tetrahedra border the channels, connecting at specific edges as shown in Fig. 7c. The compact and rigid nature of the PS4 tetrahedra is due to the short but strong P-S bonds. In contrast, the LiS4 tetrahedra are less rigid with weaker Li-S bonds. Within the channel, lithium ions are distributed between Li(4b) and Li(4c) sites, with occupancies of 0.68 and 0.32 at RT, respectively [129]. The significant number of lithium vacancies greatly enhances the lithium-ion hopping along the channel. Simultaneously, the unfilled sites and weak lithium-sulfur bonds contribute to a structure that can expand when heated [130]. The PS4 and Li(8d)S4 fillers between the channels limit thermal expansion in the ac-plane and probably restrict Li+ mobility radially [128].

Lepley et al. [131], through DFT modeling, determined that the migration along the b-axis involves a lower energy configuration associated with the interstitial site. They also identified that the pathway along the a-axis is the most challenging. The BV method by Xiao et al. [132] also suggests the presence of interstitial sites (highlighted by blue stars in Fig. 7d) along the 8d to 8d path on the b axis. The connection along the b axis is facilitated by movement in the a direction, which is indicated by the existence of a channel within the 8d-4b-8d chain (red arrows in Fig. 7d). This channel allows for migration along the c axis. They noticed a zigzag pathway along the c-axis, which is actually a series of alternating movements in the direction of the a-axis. Two types of hopping are required to achieve full migration along a axis. The first involves movement within the PS4 layer, represented by 8d-4b-8d and illustrated by red arrows in Fig. 7d. The second type describes the movement of Li+ from one PS4 layer to another, depicted as direct 8d to 8d hopping and marked by green arrows in Fig. 7e [132]. In BV calculations, forming interlayer migration channels is challenging unless the isovalue increases to 1.01 eV. This is illustrated in Fig. 7e, where extra links form between 8d and 8d sites. These connections create a complete and coherent pathway through the a axis.

The ionic conductivity of bulk γ-Li3PS4 is quite low, measuring 3 × 10−7 S cm−1 at RT (See Fig. 7f, line c). However, when the temperature increases to 195 °C, γ-Li3PS4 transforms into the high-conduction β- Li3PS4 phase (Fig. 7f, line b). This change triggers a significant rise in ionic conductivity, as shown in the Arrhenius plot [127]. However, when temperatures fall below 195 °C, the material reverts to the γ phase. Based on the extrapolation of lines b and c in Fig. 7f, the predicted conductivities for the β- and γ-Li3PS4 phases at 25 °C are 8.93 × 10−7 and 2.61 × 10−7 S cm−1, respectively. Interestingly, the nanoporous Li3PS4 created through a wet-chemical process yields an unusually high ionic conductivity of 1.6 × 10−4 S cm−1 at RT (Fig. 7f, line a). This is almost three times higher than that of crystalline Li3PS4 [106].

ISEs such as garnet oxides (LLZO) exhibit high rigidity (hardness), leading to poor particle contact and weak adhesion between particles and electrodes [133]. The interface composition between ISEs and electrodes significantly impacts the electrochemical performance of ASSBs. Interfacial resistance affects cycle stability, cell polarization, coulombic efficiency, and rate capability [19,134]. The resistance at the electrolyte-anode, electrolyte-electrolyte, and cathode-electrolyte interfaces can increase due to factors like loose physical contact, chemical or electrochemical reactions, and the presence of grain boundaries [30]. Experimental and computational techniques can effectively analyze the complexity of interfaces in ASSBs. Molecular dynamics simulations and DFT calculations offer microscopic insight into interface atomic interactions, stability, thermodynamics, and kinetics [135]. While DFT calculations can forecast the decomposition products, the experimental identification and validation of specific species, their distribution, and physical/chemical properties persist as a largely unexplored challenge. Microscopic strategies such as TEM allow a detailed examination of interface structure and ion distribution. Spectroscopic methods aid in understanding the electronic and chemical structure of interfacial components. Meanwhile, electrochemical approaches like impedance and cyclic voltammetry measurements offer insights into interface charge transfer kinetics and Li-ion diffusion processes [30].

Unlike liquid electrolytes, solid electrolytes cannot penetrate the voids and gaps in ASSBs, leading to less effective physical contact between components. Banerjee et al. [30] illustrated 13 potential interfaces that can result from anode-electrolyte, electrolyte-electrolyte, cathode-electrolyte, additive-electrolyte, and current collector-electrolyte ( Fig. 8a). These interfaces can be further grouped into voids, electrochemically formed interfaces, chemically-formed interfaces, and grain boundaries.

Despite applying high pressure (≥ 370 MPa) during cell assembly, the electrolyte and electrode remain porous, falling short of an ideal closest-packing scenario. The porosity typically varies from 10 % to 40 %, depending on the mechanical characteristics of the materials and the pressure used for the electrode and electrolyte. Voids can also develop during cell operation due to electrode breakdown, which results from crack formation in cathode particles and the dendritic growth of lithium metal. Voids can obstruct Li-ion diffusion and charge transfer, leading to greater contact resistance. They can also stimulate Li dendritic growth and increase the cell volume, thereby reducing the volumetric energy density of the cells [30].

If a chemical potential mismatch exists between a solid electrolyte and an electrode, instinctive chemical reactions may happen when they are in contact. As a result, solid electrolyte interphases (SEI) form at the anode, while cathode electrolyte interphases (CEI) form at the cathode. An effective SEI/CEI should act as a passivation layer, allowing only lithium ions to conduct and not electrons. It should also enlarge the operational voltage range of electrolytes, as depicted in Fig. 8b [22]. If the SEI/CEI acts as a mixed ionic and electronic conductor (MIEC), it will keep growing and reduce the performance of ASSBs [136]. A proven way to reduce chemical reactions is to protect the cathode/anode with relatively inert materials.

ISEs can undergo reduction and oxidation (redox) at low and high voltages, respectively, given enough contact with conductive materials such as anode, cathode, conductive additives, or current collectors. One important aspect of choosing ceramic/solid electrolyte is that its highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) needs to fall below the Fermi energy level (μc) of cathode, and its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) should exceed the Fermi energy level (μa) of anode as shown in Fig. 8c. When the Fermi energy of cathode falls below the HOMO, the ISE oxidizes at the cathode interface, forming a CEI [137]. Similarly, if the Fermi energy of the anode surpasses the LUMO, the ISE reduces at the anode interface, creating an SEI. The existence of SEI and/or CEI enhances resistance to the diffusion of Li ions and the transfer of charge across the interface. Oxide- and chloride-based ISEs have a more remarkable stability window than sulfide-based ISEs (see Fig. 8c). They remain stable at high voltages but lose stability at low voltages. Pairing electrode materials with compatible operating voltage ranges and reducing reaction speeds can decrease the electrochemical reactivity of ISEs.

A grain boundary exists when two particles with differing electrochemical potentials are in contact. This causes Li ions to move from one particle to the next, leaving behind a Li-deficient layer at the interface that significantly reduces ionic conduction. Oxide-based ISEs generally possess higher grain boundary resistance than sulfides. Grain boundary can be studied in detail using computational techniques to understand the electronic, structural, and ion conduction properties of ISEs. Quirk and Dawson [138] employed computational (first-principles calculations) to study the grain boundary of anti-perovskite oxide. Their results demonstrated that grain boundaries reduce the band gap, disrupt fast diffusion pathways, lower Li-ion conductivity, and increase reorientation of polyanions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategies to improve interface and conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of the cathode materials and ISE significantly influences the electrochemical performance and interfacial contact of ASSBs. Strauss et al. [139] discovered that in a carbon-free composite cathode with Li3PS4, NMC622 particles under 10 μm had a greater capacity than the larger ones. A composite containing smaller particles has a significantly higher electronic conductivity (more than two orders of magnitude) than composites from larger particles. This is due to quicker kinetics and better use of the cathode material.

Using a protective inert coating layer effectively prevents electrochemical/chemical reactions between electrodes and ISEs, thus minimizing the space charge layer effect and reducing interfacial resistance. An appropriate coating layer for ASSBs should possess the following characteristics: (i) Phase stability or enhanced thermodynamic stability [140], (ii) chemical compatibility with the cathode and ISE [141], (iii) electrochemical stability within the cathode’s operating voltage range to avoid transition metal redox activity [142], (iv) mechanical elasticity for strain accommodation to prevent cracking [143], and (v) ionic conductivity to facilitate Li ion transport across the layer, enhancing the performance rate of ASSBs (Fig. 8d) [144]. The interface between lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) and sulfide-based or oxide-based ISEs can be significantly enhanced with a coating layer applied before heating/annealing. Kato et al. [77] observed that when a thin niobium layer was added between the LCO and LLZO, the interfacial resistance was reduced from 2600 Ω/cm2 to 150 Ω/cm2. Ohta et al. [145] discovered that applying a Li4Ti5O12 coating on LCO diminishes the initial slope seen before charging, likely caused by a space charge layer formation. A similar observation was made with thiophosphate-based LPS. By applying a specific LTO thickness, the slope can be removed [146].

Through in-situ XPS coupled with time-resolved electrochemical measurements, Wenzel et al. [147] analyzed the interface stability between LISICON-like LGPS and Li-metal anode. Their analysis revealed the formation of interphases made up of Li3P, Li2S, and Li-Ge alloy at the CE-Li anode. Oh et al. [148] also proposed that forming an SEI-like layer containing Li2S at the LGPS/Li-metal interface could prevent further LGPS decomposition. The same Li2S interphase was also discovered between Li7P3S11 and Li-metal [149]. However, the chemical instability of ISEs with Li-metal was also observed in Li4SnS4 due to the reduction of tin. This issue can be addressed in two ways: i) stabilizing the Li-metal electrode or altering the ISEs surface; ii) creating a Li-M alloy where M represents elements like In, Si, Al, etc. Sahu et al. [150] discovered that 3LiBH4·LiI effectively enhances the compatibility of Li4SnS4 with Li-metal by serving as a protective coating.

Deformability is crucial, as it directly corresponds to the densification capacity of the ISEs. Oxide-based ISEs are traditionally rigid and require high-temperature annealing to decrease grain boundaries. However, this process is energy-consuming, expensive, and could trigger unwanted side reactions with electrode materials. Alternatively, sulfide-based ISEs are more flexible. They can be densified at RT through simple cold-pressing. This makes them more appealing from a mechanical and processing standpoint, as it aids in creating a better electrolyte-electrode interface [151]. ISEs densification is promoted by atomic and polyhedral diffusion, with rapid diffusion aiding the merging of grain boundaries. In Li2S:P2S5-based ISEs, PS4 3- polyhedral and Li-ions can diffuse along these grain boundaries, filling voids or gaps between particles when pressure is applied. This ability to diffuse is dependent on the types and strengths of bonds within the ISE structure. However, oxide-based glassy ISEs (75 % Li2O:25 % P2O5) are significantly less adaptable compared to their sulfide glass counterparts (75 %Li2S:25 % P2S5). This is due to the stronger Li–O and P–O bonds than the weaker Li–S and P–S bonds [152,153]. The elastic modulus, or Young's Modulus, is also a significant property of ASSBs as it influences volume changes during the cycling process [154]. An ISE with a high Young's Modulus cannot effectively handle the stress induced by these volume changes, which can cause cracks at the interface. The cracks increase the interfacial contact resistance by reducing the contact area between the ISE and electrode, thus slowing reaction rates and reducing the Coulombic efficiency (CE). Typically, sulfide-based ISEs have Young's modulus of 18–25 GPa, which is significantly less than oxide-based ISEs (160–180 GPa). Lower values enable the ISE to adapt more effectively to stress caused by changes in cathode volume [155].

Solid state NMR is an effective technique for examining ion movement and the local structure of materials. Being a quadrupolar nucleus (I = 3/2), the static 7Li NMR signals rely on the relationship between the electrical quadrupole moment of Li-ion and the electric field gradient (EFG) at its position. The EFG is generated by the neighboring ions surrounding lithium-ion, making the static spectra responsive to lattice structure changes and movements. Through external magnetic field gradient, the diffusion coefficient can be directly determined [157]. Typically, the static 7Li NMR consists of a central line, resulting from the -1/2 ↔ 1/2 transition, and two satellite lines, resulting from the 1/2 ↔ 3/2 and -1/2 ↔ 3/2 transitions relating to the electric quadrupolar interactions [158]. The spin-lattice relaxation time (T 1) evaluates local charge-density fluctuations resulting from ionic motion. The T 1, T 2 (spin-spin relaxation time), and T 1 ρ (spin-lattice relaxation in the rotating frame time) depend on temperature and the sample composition. The intensity of T 1 and T 2, along with the nuclear resonance line width, provide information on diffusion and the interaction strength between nuclei and the local environment [82].

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR line-shape and spin-lattice relaxation analysis
ce:section.ce:para: NMR line shapes and relaxation measurements efficiently examine slow and rapid bulk ion movements in ISEs. Epp et al. [18] utilized 7Li NMR to observe key Li-ion jump processes at an atomic level closely. NMR relaxation exposed a mix of ultra-fast and slower diffusion processes in NASICON-type polycrystalline lithium titanium aluminum phosphate Li1.5Al0.5Ti1.5(PO4)3 (LATP). Through NMR line-shape analysis, Li ion dynamics were revealed by averaging the dipolar magnetic and electric quadrupolar interactions (See Fig. 9a). At low temperatures, these dynamics consisted of a dipolar broadened NMR central line, optimally represented through a Voigt profile and a broad quadrupole component with an approximate width of 24 kHz at 185 K (Red echo spectrum in Fig. 9a). As the temperature increased, the width of the central line decreased because of the averaged dipole-dipole interactions. Besides, sharp satellite intensities replaced the broad quadrupole component when the temperature exceeded 200 K. By 300 K, the uniquely shaped powder pattern fully formed, directly indicating fast Li diffusivity. The quadrupole coupling constant δQ (7Li) for singularities at 296 K is approximately 45.4 kHz. δQ value slightly depends on temperature, considering diffusion processes activate progressively as temperature increases (see Fig. 9a). This is commonly referred to as NMR line narrowing in existing literature [159]. Satellite signals can only be completely averaged and vanish in materials with numerous electrically distinct Li sites, such as amorphous materials. Fig. 9b presents a plot of the line width (full width at half maximum, FWHM) of the central NMR line as a function of temperature (T), showing that substantial motion line narrowing begins below 140 K. The line narrowing at temperatures as low as 150 K indicates a rapid Li-ion exchange among the diverse Li sites in LATP.

The exceptional Li diffusivity, which results in homonuclear dipole-dipole averaging, is verified by the spin–lattice relaxation (SLR) NMR. SLR data is utilized to measure the diffusion processes with respect to activation energies and diffusion coefficients [160]. Fig. 9c is the Arrhenius representation of the 7Li NMR spin-lattice relaxation rates, R 1 and R 1ρ, of LATP examined in the laboratory (filled sphere) and the rotating frame of reference (unfilled sphere), respectively. From the relaxation (diffusion-induced) rate peaks, the jump rates at the maxima temperatures are approximately 108 s−1 and 104 s−1, depending on whether the measurements were taken in the laboratory or a rotating frame. Fig. 9c indicates three separate relaxation processes within the given temperature range, distinguished by the maxima labeled A, B, and C. The relaxation process indicated by maximum C is only partially visible in the rotating frame at temperatures above 600 K. This peak could possibly represent Li motion along a unique diffusion pathway that is only activated at high temperatures. Alternatively, it could signify the rotational dynamics of the [PO4] or [TiO6] polyhedra. On the other hand, the peak relaxation rates A and B in both reference frames are due to the translational hopping of Li+. These processes may involve jumps between M1 and M3 sites or include other interstitial sites. The addition of Al3+ leads to extra Li ions occupying M3 sites while facilitating the displacement of Li+ ions from M1 towards Al sites. This may lead to an easier or smoother jump process than Al-free LTP, which involves hopping between M1 and M2 sites. As demonstrated by Lang et al. [47], extra Li ions tend to gather around the Al centers. This trapping effect can initiate diffusion processes at higher temperatures.

Fig. 9d compares the 7Li NMR SLR rates R1ρ between LATP and other fast-conducting ISEs (garnet-type cLLZTO and sulfide/argyrodite-type Li6PS5Br). Spin-lock NMR relaxometry was utilized to compare the diffusion rates of the three samples (cLLZTO, LATP, and LPSB). The peak maxima of the three samples directly indicate the degree of Li ion diffusivity, considering the rates were compared at equivalent locking frequencies [161]. The further the peak shifted towards lower temperatures, the greater the mobility of the Li-ion. The order of Li-ion diffusivity was Li6PS5Br (Tmax = 167 K, 0.20 eV) > LATP (Tmax = 214 K, 0.16 eV) > cLLZTO (Tmax = 307 K, 0.34 eV). Therefore, phosphate clearly shows superior Li-ion transport properties compared to garnet oxide.

7Li pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR is used to characterize Li-ion diffusivity in ISEs. This technique is sensitive to Li-ion dynamics on a macroscopic scale and unaffected by less conductive impurities [162]. Mo et al. [111] calculated the theoretical diffusion coefficients of LGPS along the c-axis and in the ab-plane using a DFT MD simulation between 600 K and 1500 K ( Fig. 10a). The diffusion coefficient was calculated from the average mean square displacement of Li atoms over time. Due to fast Li diffusion, converging Li+ diffusion along the c and ab axis was reached after 40 ps of MD simulations. The overall activation barrier for Li diffusion in LGPS was 0.21 eV.

Kuhn et al. [162] performed experimental 7Li PFG NMR of Li7GePS8 and Li10GeP2S12 (Fig. 10b, i). The self-diffusion coefficients showed activation energies of 0.21 eV for Li10GeP2S12 and 0.23 eV for Li7GePS8, indicating that Li diffusivity is mainly unaffected by the Ge/P ratio within the studied compositional range. Fig. 10b, ii shows the 31P NMR spectra of Li7GePS8 and Li10GeP2S12. Li7GePS8 shows a 2:1 ratio of the two 31P lines from tetragonal LGPS, while both 31P lines of Li10GeP2S12 have equal intensity. Additionally, both samples contain small amounts of impurities from the orthorhombic modification, visible as a third line in the spectra (asterisk).

Kuhn et al. [163] further characterized the Li diffusivity of Li10SnP2S12 and Li11Si2PS12 using 7Li PFG NMR. The obtained Li tracer-diffusion coefficients are shown in Fig. 10c and compared with those for Li10GeP2S12 [162]. The diffusivity of Li10SnP2S12 is slightly lower than Li10GeP2S12, with a higher activation energy (0.23 eV vs. 0.21 eV). In contrast, Li11Si2PS12 has higher diffusivity than Li10GeP2S12 and lower activation energy (0.19 eV vs. 0.21 eV). The difference in self-diffusion coefficients is attributed to the unit cell volume and Li diffusivity in tetragonal LGPS-type materials. LSiPS exhibits the smallest unit cell, which shows enhanced diffusivity, while LSnPS possesses the largest unit cell, leading to the lowest diffusivity.

2D exchange spectroscopy (2D EXSY) NMR allows for the precise observation of the spontaneous transfer of Li+ (charge transfer reaction) between different environments [164]. Wang et al. [165] researched lithium transport mechanisms in garnet-type cubic LLZO using 2D EXSY NMR. Their high-resolution NMR results revealed that Li-ions are situated in tetragonal (24d), octahedral (48 g), and off-centered octahedral (96 h) sites. The 96 h sites emerge due to the distortion of the 48 g sites. The tetrahedral 24d sites have a 30–40 % Li occupancy rate, and the total Li quantities at the octahedral sites (48 g and 96 h) are approximately equal. Fig. 11a represents a 1D 6Li MAS NMR spectra of Li7–2x-3y Al y La3Zr2–x W x O12 (0 ≤ x ≤ 1). The spectra reveal two slightly overlapping peaks. These can be attributed to the lithium ions positioned at the tetrahedral (0.8 ppm) and octahedral (1.9 ppm) sites [166]. The spin-lattice relaxation time (T 1) was characterized using the saturation recovery method. Li at the octahedral (48 g) sites exhibited a shorter T 1 value (1.0–5.0 s), suggesting higher mobility. Conversely, Li at the tetrahedral (24d) sites demonstrated a longer T 1 (14–28 s), indicating slower mobility [157]. By analyzing the T 1 difference, the strong signals from the octahedral sites were suppressed. This was achieved through integrating inversion recovery with a specified recovery time. The method allowed the shorter T 1 component (the 6Li signals from octahedral sites) to relax to null, while the longer T 1 component (the 6Li signals from tetrahedral sites) remained in the -z axis [167].

Fig. 11b displays the 6Li spectrum after the strong signals are suppressed at 1.9 ppm. Notably, there is a hidden peak at 1.6 ppm, previously buried in Fig. 11a. This peak is expected to have a T 1 value similar to the one at 0.8 ppm. The newly discovered peak at 1.6 ppm, likely due to its very weak signal intensity, probably represents lithium ions located at the distorted octahedral sites (96 h). Meanwhile, the 1.9 ppm peak is likely associated with lithium at the 48 g sites. Fig. 11c presents the 2D 6Li–6Li exchange spectrum with a 10-second mixing time after the strong signals are suppressed at 1.9 ppm. A cross-peak at (ω1 = 0.8, ω2 = 1.6) ppm clearly indicates a correlation between the octahedral (96 h) and tetrahedral (24d) sites. This suggests that the Li+ ions move back and forth between neighboring octahedral and tetrahedral sites. Considering that the 96 h sites in the crystal structure of garnet phase are situated between the 48 g and 24d sites, these results verify that the Li+ ions in the 24d tetrahedral sites contribute to the lithium migration pathway. This further suggests that the Li+ ions move along the 24d–96h–48g–96h–24d sequence [165].

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR analysis at the electrode-ISE interface
ce:section.ce:para: Kuhn et al. [159] studied Li self-diffusivity in crystalline lithium silicides Li12Si7, potential anode materials, by 7Li NMR SLR measurements. Static 7Li NMR spectroscopy (on non-rotating samples) was employed to analyze microscopic Li diffusion parameters, while variable-temperature 7Li NMR spin-lattice relaxation analyses were used to provide information on the dynamic parameters such as activation enthalpies and diffusive jump processes. Meanwhile, frequency-dependent NMR SLR rate calculations offered valuable insights into the dimensionality of the absolute jump rates. Fig. 12a shows 7Li NMR spectra between 125 and 258 K at resonance frequency (ω0/2π) = 155.4 MHz and 723 K at 77.7 MHz. The broad and complex shaped line at 125 K can be attributed to (i) the chemical shift anisotropies, (ii) 7Li unaveraged dipole-dipole interactions, and (iii) the interactions between the quadrupole moment and the electric field gradients at different Li sites within the Li12Si7 crystal structure. As the temperature rises, the thermally activated 7Li ions motion gradually averages out the dipole-dipole interactions. When the Li jump rate (τ–1) is similar to the spectral width Δωrl/2π, substantial line narrowing occurs.

The motional narrowing onset in Li12Si7 was observed at an exceptionally low temperature of approximately 130 K, as depicted in Fig. 12b. The Li jump rate can be roughly estimated at the corresponding temperature using the relation τ–1 ≈ Δωrl. The Li jump rate was approximately 105 s–1 at 150 K, and it increased to 109 s–1 at 425 K, suggesting a minimal activation energy of 0.18 eV. The 7Li NMR spectra at 188, 258, and 723 K exhibited the characteristic powder spectrum shape for a spin 3/2 nucleus, with a central transition line and quadrupolar satellite intensities Fig. 12a. The mean quadrupolar coupling constants δQ can be calculated from the relevant spectral distances Δωsat/2π (indicated by vertical red lines on the singularities of the powder pattern) using the formula δQ = 2 × Δωsat/2π. Three distinct jump processes were successively triggered in the crystalline Li12Si7, each resulting in a unique mean coupling constant δQ. The constants δQ were identified as 47.4 kHz, 10.8 kHz, and 5.6 kHz at 188 K, 258 K, and 723 K, respectively [159].

NMR SLR measurements provided greater clarity on the lithium dynamics of crystalline Li12Si7. Fig. 12c displays Arrhenius plots for 7Li NMR SLR rates in both laboratory frame of reference (T 1 –1) and rotating frame of reference (T 1 ρ –1). These rates were measured at various resonance (ω0/2π = 77.7 and 155.4 MHz) and locking (ω1/2π = 12.4 and 39.0 kHz) frequencies. The NMR SLR here is solely triggered by local shifts in magnetic dipolar or electric quadrupolar interactions resulting from the movement of Li spins. The findings demonstrated that the ultrafast diffusion process seen in Li12Si7 belonged to a low-dimensional type (quasi 1D). The T 1 –1 and low-temperature maxima of the T 1 ρ –1 curves in Fig. 12c correspond to this ultrafast diffusion process, which has a half activation energy of 0.09 eV. Besides the quasi-1D ultrafast dynamic process in Li12Si7, the T 1 ρ –1(1/T) data also indicates two other 3D jump processes at high temperatures (HT). The first 3D diffusion process at 3.5 K−1 is slower than the quasi-1D process and is observed near RT with an activation energy of 0.32 eV. It likely causes the subtle shoulder seen at 1000/T ≈ 1.5 K–1 on the HT flanks of the rate peak T 1 –1 recorded at 77.7 MHz, as indicated in Fig. 12c. The secondary, slower 3D diffusive process, with the highest activation energy of 0.55 eV, is only observable in the T 1 ρ –1(1/T) data and resembles self-diffusion in lithium metal [168].

Yu et al. [169] conducted a study on the transportation of lithium-ion across the interface of the argyrodite Li6PS5Br SE and the Li2S sulfide-based cathode. This study aimed to understand how the battery cycle and electrode preparation affected the lithium-ion transfer across the interface. According to their XRD refinement and TEM results, the particle sizes of Li2S and Li6PS5Br were 38 nm and 23 nm, respectively, which align with EDX measurements. All mixtures were prepared with a 1:1 mass ratio of the Li6PS5Br SE to the Li2S cathode material. Fig. 12d illustrates the nanoscale of a Li2S cathode from 120 nm in mixture I to 38 nm in mixture III. The capacity retention was measured using galvanostatic charging. Ball-milled nanosized Li2S exhibited superior discharge capacity and cyclability, achieving 628 mAh g−1 compared to 56 mAh g−1 for large-sized Li2S. The subpar (dis)charge capacity in the mixture I is due to the low electronic and ionic conductivity of Li2S. The improved performance in mixture II is due to minimized kinetic limitations caused by shorter charge transport distances between Li6PS5Br and Li2S. However, the superior cycling performance in mixture III is attributed to an increased and better Li2S-Li6PS5Br interface created through high-energy ball milling.

Fig. 12e shows the 1D 7Li MAS NMR spectra of the Li6PS5Br–Li2S for mixtures I, II, and III. MAS balanced out anisotropic interactions like chemical shift anisotropy, dipolar, and first-order quadrupolar in the spectra. The chemical shift difference between Li in Li6PS5Br and Li2S allowed the differentiation of lithium ions in Li2S and Li6PS5Br phases, permitting the performance of 2D EXSY NMR experiments. 2D EXSY NMR allows for the precise observation of the spontaneous transfer of LI+ (charge transfer reaction) between different environments [164]. The 2D EXSY NMR spectra in Fig. 12 f depicts a broad homogeneous resonance for 7Li in Li2S, compared to a star-shaped resonance for 7Li in Li6PS5Br. No significant off-diagonal cross-peak intensity is observed in either mixture I or II, suggesting a minimal exchange over the Li6PS5Br–Li2S interface. In Mixture III, the off-diagonal cross-peaks emerge at tmix = 10 ms and are most prominent at tmix = 100 ms. This represents the exchange of lithium ions from Li6PS5Br to Li2S and vice versa. No off-diagonal signal is detected in mixture III at tmix = 100 µs because the mixing time is insufficient for lithium ions to diffuse between Li2S and Li6PS5Br. It is worth noting that the star shape of the 7Li resonance in Li6PS5Br results from the two-dimensional Fourier transform of its Lorentzian shape, which is due to the high mobility of lithium ions within the Li6PS5Br phase.

While ssNMR is widely used to study the local structure, correlations, phase transformation, and dynamics for materials characterization, its low sensitivity limits its use. The low signal intensity in NMR is due to the small population difference between nuclear spin states. Despite advanced techniques like cross-polarization (CP) to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and reduce experimental time, it remains challenging to obtain information on species present in low quantities (e.g., surface species, active sites, defects) or isotopes with low gyromagnetic ratios (15N, 35Cl, 17O, 43Ca, etc.), low natural abundance (13C, 17O, 29Si, etc.), or broad line shapes due to anisotropic chemical shielding and quadrupolar interactions (e.g., 6Li, 27Al, etc.) [170]. The low sensitivity of NMR can be improved through hyperpolarization techniques like dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP).

DNP works by transferring the high spin polarization of unpaired electrons to surrounding nuclear spins through microwave irradiation, boosting NMR sensitivity by several orders of magnitude [171]. To apply DNP for materials characterization, three components are required: (a) a polarizing agent (PA) providing unpaired electrons, which can be added externally (exogenous) or introduced during/after synthesis (endogenous) ( Fig. 13a); (b) a high-frequency microwave source (a gyrotron) to saturate electron transitions at high magnetic fields; and (c) a magic angle spinning (MAS) cryoprobes for high-resolution spinning at cryogenic temperatures, to slow electron relaxation and enhance DNP efficiency [170,172]. The solid, cross and Overhauser effects are three main mechanisms of hyperpolarization through DNP [173].

Characterizing interface atoms is challenging in battery studies because of the limited number of surface atoms and low sensitivity of NMR. DNP surface-enhanced NMR spectroscopy (DNP-SENS) can address this, thus enabling the study of the structure and composition of the electrode-electrolyte interface. In DNP-SENS, the sample is treated with a small amount of radical solution, such as nitroxide biradicals, to uniformly wet the surface [174]. Polarization is then transferred to surface nuclei (Fig. 13b), and due to their proximity to the PAs, surface atoms accumulate hyperpolarization more quickly than bulk atoms, enhancing their spectral signals (Fig. 13c, red). In suspension, the sample is dissolved in a radical-containing solution and then cooled to ∼100 K before DNP enhances its signal [175]. In mesoporous materials, the porous volume is filled with a radical-containing solution, while in bulk materials, the surface is wetted with PAs (Fig. 13d) [174].

DNP-SENS cannot polarize bulk nuclei in materials like ceramics, oxides, phosphates, and silicates due to their low abundance, receptivity, and significant distance from the surface. Unlike organic materials, inorganic materials lack high-γ 1H nuclei for efficient polarization transfer, prompting recent suggestions for alternative polarization sources and transfer methods [170]. However, Björgvinsdóttir et al. [176] showed that nuclei with moderate gyromagnetic ratios (γ) and natural abundances (NA), such as 29Si, 6Li, 119Sn, and 113Cd, can transfer polarization to the bulk nuclei. Using the DNP-SENS approach, surface nuclei are hyperpolarized first, and then a pulse cooling method (multiple-contact CP) is used to transfer the polarization to the bulk. This approach differs from conventional DNP, which utilizes a single CP excitation pulse to detect signals from hyperpolarization PAs (wetting phase) [177].

Fig. 13g shows DNP-enhanced 1H→119Sn CPMAS spectra of SnO2 acquired at 9.4 T, 8 kHz MAS, and 100 K. Above the spectrum, the pulse sequence is shown, which allows 119Sn spin diffusion during the mixing time between CP and detection (τz). Under DNP conditions, the CP transfers magnetization from hyperpolarized protons (1H) to 119Sn nuclei at the surface, resulting in a 350-fold increase in surface polarization. At τz = 0, peaks from both surface (−590 ppm and −615 ppm) and bulk (−603 ppm) sites are observed. As τz increases, the surface signal decreases, and the bulk signal builds, reaching a maximum at τz = 60 s. This shows spin diffusion occurs from the surface to the bulk [176].

Another approach is to utilize radicals present in the material or generated through chemical or physical treatments to serve as endogenous PAs. Carnahan et al. [178] showed that γ-irradiation creates stable radicals in inorganic solids such as fused quartz (SiO2). Ionizing radiation such as γ-rays are known to develop stable radicals [179]. In Fig. 13e, DNP-SENS polarizes dangling 1H spins, and 1H→29Si CP polarizes surface 29Si spins (right). Relayed DNP uses 29Si spin diffusion to polarize core 29Si (middle). DNP on γ-irradiated SiO2 polarizes core 29Si (left), which results in signal enhancement (ε) (Fig. 13f). SiO2 was γ-irradiated using a 60Co source with doses ranging from 100 to 400 kGy to enhance the core 29Si spins, achieving ε of ⁓ 400. Doping metal centers with moderate NA and γ (e.g., 29Si, 119Sn, and 113Cd) to organic solids [180] or inorganic materials [181] can act as endogenous PAs and be used to hyperpolarize nuclei in the bulk core. This approach enables the study of bulk crystals and interfaces, considering that the metal ions DNP (MIDNP) are used as endogenous PAs for MAS DNP [182]. Only a few paramagnetic MIDNP have been reported, including Fe(III), Cr(III), Mn(II), Gd(III), and V(IV).
