A good MIDNP requires a long T 1e (electron relaxation time) and an EPR line within the magnetic field range of the DNP system [170].

High-field MAS DNP has become crucial for studying the surface chemistry of electrode materials, which affect battery performance. Using MAS DNP NMR, Leskes et al. [183], for the first time in 2017, enhanced ssNMR sensitivity to the outer SEI layer, enabling the detection of natural abundance 13C spectra within reasonable timeframes. This was followed by Jin et al. [184] employing 29Si DNP NMR to probe the SEI-Si interface, revealing that organosiloxanes form during cycling and are covalently bonded to the Si anode surface. This investigation suggested that the polymeric structure of the SEI and its adhesion to redox-active materials are key to electrochemical performance. Since then, this technique has been widely used to explore SEI and CEI structures, tracking changes during cycling, and examine thin coating at the interfacial regions. Hestenes et al. [185] utilized 1H→13C CPMAS DNP NMR to investigate the CEI on Li2RuO3/LP30/Li cell cycled at C/10 and disassembled after the 27th cycle at 2.0 V discharge (Fig. 13h). The analysis showed that the CEI contained electrolyte decomposition products, including Li alkyl carbonates/polycarbonates at 160 ppm and Li acetate at 173 ppm. Further analysis using 2D 1H-13C HETCOR (heteronuclear correlation) DNP NMR revealed the sensitivity and detailed structural assignments of the thin (few nanometers) CEI on Li2RuO3, confirming the presence of Li acetate and carbonates (Fig. 13i).

This review concentrates on the four primary ISEs: halides, phosphates (NASICON), sulfides (LISICON, LGPS, and argyrodite-type), and oxides (perovskite, anti-perovskite, and garnet). It extensively highlights how factors like chemical composition, crystal structure, point defects, and diffusion types and pathways contribute to their ionic transport mechanisms and interface engineering. The structure, properties, energy barriers, and interface of ISEs can be determined by combining experimental characterization techniques (like TEM, XRD, XPS, and SEM) and calculation methods (DFT, BV, and VTF). The solid-state NMR is a powerful method for studying ionic transport mechanisms in ISEs. The review emphasizes using NMR line shape and width analysis to investigate Li ion dynamics, NMR relaxometry to examine diffusion process mechanisms involving slow and rapid bulk ion movements in ASSBs, PFG NMR to study ion diffusivity, and 2D EXSY to investigate Li-ion exchange at the interfaces. Due to NMR’s low sensitivity and few surface atoms, dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) is highlighted as a technique to enhance sensitivity for studying the electrode-electrolyte interface.

ISEs possess desirable properties like a broad electrochemical window (⁓ 6 V vs. Li∣Li+), enhanced ionic conductivity at higher temperatures, better mechanical and thermal stability, and fewer safety risks regarding flammability and leakages. However, their poor ionic conductivity and high interfacial resistance at RT have hindered their commercialization. Factors such as grain boundaries, cracks or voids, chemical and electrochemical reactions, and diffusion pathways within the ISEs affect ionic conductivity. Ionic conductivity can be enhanced through various methods, such as using thinner SE membranes, operating the ASSB at higher temperatures, modifying grain boundaries by adding a secondary phase to create an amorphous boundary layer, and optimizing the synthesis conditions of ISEs through grain size control, sintering, and doping with elements like aluminum.

Interfaces result from various interactions, including anode-electrolyte, electrolyte-electrolyte, cathode-electrolyte, additive-electrolyte, and current collector-electrolyte. These interfaces can be categorized into voids, electrochemically and chemically formed interfaces, and grain boundaries. A suitable approach to address the interfacial issue in ISEs is to minimize the particle size of both ISE and electrodes using ball milling. This increases the surface contact between ISE and electrodes, shortens the Li diffusion length, and enhances charge transfer dynamics at the interface. Another method involves using a protective inert coating layer at the ISE-electrode interface. This layer effectively inhibits electrochemical and chemical reactions between electrodes and counter electrodes, thus reducing the space charge layer effect and lowering interfacial resistance. Other techniques can involve the use of flexible ISEs with a low elastic or Young's modulus to prevent cracks and voids when pressure is applied during ASSB assembly, and pairing electrode materials with compatible operating voltage ranges to decrease the electro-chemical reactivity of ISEs.

While significant advancements have been made with ISEs, several key issues still need addressing. First, despite ceramic electrolytes (phosphates and oxides) being stable against temperature, moisture, and air, further research on their fabrication, processing, and handling is needed. Production of uniform films over larger areas, reducing sintering temperatures and thickness, will expand the range of these materials for next-generation battery development.

Second, the problem associated with moisture sensitivity in sulfide- and halide-based ISEs should be resolved for easy handling in a dry room. Apart from surface coatings and structural adjustments, creating humidity-tolerant sulfide and halide-based ISEs with innate moisture stability is beneficial. Developing intrinsically hydrophobic SEs can be utilized in open-system applications such as metal-air batteries, which offer high energy density.

Third, ISEs require high production costs for large-scale applications. Developing innovative, affordable, and environmentally friendly production methods is crucial for successful commercialization. This could be achieved by using low-cost materials, replacing expensive elements with cheaper ones, or creating more cost-effective manufacturing processes.

Fourth, grain boundary predominantly affects oxide-based ISEs like garnet and perovskite. This results in poor wetting behavior, instability against electrodes, unstable phase lattice, and low grain-boundary conductivity (<10−5 S cm−1). It is necessary to investigate improved doping strategies to modify the phase structure, vacancy concentration, Li-ion content, ion occupancy, and skeleton structure in order to enhance the lithium-ion migration pathways in garnet-based ISEs. For perovskite-based ISEs, strategies could include doping the A site with large rare-earth, reducing the B-O interatomic distance by substituting B sites with elements of smaller ionic radius like aluminum, or creating perovskite-based ISEs in an amorphous form.

Fifth, various stable structural and chemical candidates for ISE materials can be screened using advanced computer techniques such as MD simulations and calculations, which can aid in creating fitting models and metrics to identify fast ion conductors. The novel ion conductors should be stable against electrolyte decomposition, air and moisture contamination, and capable of resolving interface instability with electrodes.

Sixth, ssNMR is a powerful tool for studying atomic structure, ion diffusion, correlations among materials by techniques such as HETCOR, and phase transformation (crystallinity-to-amorphous) through variable temperature NMR experiments. It should be applied to future ISEs studies to understand molecular structure and ion conductivity mechanisms better. To improve the low sensitivity of ssNMR, more DNP studies are needed to enable the analysis of electrode-electrolyte interfaces in batteries. Researchers can also couple DNP MAS NMR with other surface characterization techniques like XPS and Raman spectroscopy for a comprehensive understanding of surface chemistry at the interface, which is a core focus of ISEs.
